summaries.text: Subject Emil Carlebach of Frankfurt a.Main submitted a ten-page German report documenting events at Buchenwald between Spring 1938 and April 11, 1945. The witness served as a Blockältester and active member of the Jewish Group within the camp's Antifascist Organisation. The report contains allegations of systematic ill-treatment, mass murder, and illicit camp-to-camp deportation transports. Furthermore, it details organized resistance activities that neutralized internal camp antisemitism and saved numerous lives. Specific references are made to Ernst Heilmann, the use of guard dogs, prisoner insanity, Dutch Jews, and bribery of officials.

The dossier names Kurt Eisner junior (page 5) in connection with Buchenwald operations beginning in 1938. Initial camp management was reportedly dominated by criminal kapos cooperating with the SS. Key camp personnel identified include Camp-commander Koch, Oberscharführer Sommer, and Lagerarzt Dr. Hoven. The document lists various criminal activities and resistance operations, including medical experiments, mutiny, sabotage, and instances of non-Jews assisting Jewish inmates. Auxiliary geographic sites linked to the investigation include Auschwitz, Camp Gusen (Mauthausen), Magdeburg, and Zeist.

Testimony reveals that approximately 1,000 prisoners were subjected to severe deprivation in Buchenwald's initial phase. Housed in a cowshed, their daily food ration was restricted to 300 grams of bread and 3/4 litre of watery soup, which was frequently embezzled by supervising kapos. Prisoners were forced to perform manual labor for 14 to 15 hours a day, leaving only 5 hours for sleep. The witness asserts a pattern of systematic harassment, particularly in the quarry, where three to four prisoners daily were coerced to cross the security cordon and shot by guards under the pretext of preventing escape.

On September 23 and 24, 1938, two transports of approximately 1,000 Jewish men each arrived at Buchenwald, which was then operating as a liquidation camp. The majority of the deportees were arrested in Vienna following the Austrian occupation. German anti-fascist prisoners organized a campaign to purge criminal elements from supervisor roles. This resistance effort succeeded in improving security for Jewish prisoners, leading to an official declaration by the Kommandant on January 30 that Jewish prisoners would henceforth be supervised by Jewish overseers.

Evidence indicates that Jewish prisoners were treated as pariahs, subjected to lethal working hours and inadequate care, resulting in a high mortality rate. On November 9, 1938, over 12,000 Jews were interned during the 'Rath campaign'. The witness reports that SS guards, armed with whips and clubs, assaulted the deportees in the streets of Weimar prior to their arrival. Visual evidence at the scene included bloodied clothing, abandoned luggage, and severely injured victims lying in the road.

Upon arrival, 12,000 prisoners were crammed into five emergency barracks lacking latrines, straw mattresses, blankets, or basic sanitation. SS personnel used whips and pistols to drive the crowd, causing mass panic on the first night. Selected prisoners were chained hand-to-hand in circles and subjected to attacks by guard dogs. Seventy individuals became insane during the night, were held in a wooden shed, and were subsequently beaten in the cells by Oberscharführer Sommer. The SS target selection focused heavily on wealthy business owners to facilitate systemic extortion.

The testimony outlines widespread financial corruption by SS staff. Camp administrators used loudspeakers to extort assets, promising priority release to prisoners who surrendered houses or vehicles. SS personnel and criminal accomplices extorted thousands of Marks daily using pretexts like damage or release fees. The campaign concluded with a mass release after costing hundreds of prisoner lives, during which millions in assets passed through the camp. This financial corruption compromised the SS command structure, prompting Kommandant Koch to replace criminal kapos with political prisoners to regain control.

In September 1939, the Kommandant ordered a ban on medical treatment for Jewish prisoners, though hospital staff covertly ignored the directive. Shortly thereafter, approximately 2,500 stateless Jews from Vienna and occupied eastern territories were brought to the parade ground and housed behind barbed wire in five large tents. This sub-camp was run by SS-Scharführers Blank and Hinkelmann, who declared their intent to liquidate all inmates. The transport included residents of old age homes and boarding schools, prompting Camp Leader Hüttig to remark on the unusual nature of their free status prior to arrest.

The witness documents systematic murders in the parade ground enclosure through starvation, lack of clothing, and freezing temperatures, resulting in sixty deaths per day during winter. SS-Scharführers Blank, Hinkelmann, and Sommer actively participated, including distributing poisoned food. The prisoner resistance countered by targeting collaborator inmates who carried out SS orders. They successfully removed the foreman of the sub-camp food detail, replacing him with Kurt Posener to facilitate illicit food distribution.

Kurt Posener risked his life to smuggle supplementary food into the sub-camp. The resistance also installed Max Vulkan as clerk to offset the actions of dangerous collaborators Wolf and Rosenbaum. In early 1940, Walter Krämer, the prisoner in charge of the camp sick bay, convinced camp physicians to evacuate the sub-camp on the grounds that it was an infectious threat to the main camp and nearby villages. Using an illegal ambulance service established by hospital staff, the resistance successfully evacuated approximately 500 surviving prisoners into the main camp.

Following the Munich beer hall assassination attempt, SS personnel locked Buchenwald's Jewish prisoners in darkened barracks on November 9, 1939. An SS detail led by Scharführers Blank and Jännisch selected 21 young Jewish men and shot them in the quarry under the pretext of an escape attempt. The remaining prisoners were denied food for three days. This was followed by a camp-wide five-day food deprivation order issued on the pretext of a stolen pig. Survivors of the sub-camp were subsequently assigned to lethal rock-breaking details in the quarry.

SS-Scharführers Blank and Hinkelmann directed the quarry workgroups where prisoners were systematically beaten and shot. On May 3, 1940, Rudi Arndt, a Jewish Berlin antifascist and leader of Block 22, was denounced, removed from his post, and murdered in the quarry. Arndt, along with released camp elder Ernst Frommhold, had led the camp's organized resistance. Max Vulkan was murdered in the same manner fourteen days later. The witness reports having personally overheard the collaborator Wolf threaten Vulkan with death if he spoke about events in the sub-camp.

In the summer of 1940, a delegation comprising Kommandant Koch, camp leader Schobert, and camp doctor Florstadt visited the camp's darning station. They ordered all Jewish prisoners working there to be transferred to the quarry, where Austrian prisoner Hans Kunke was shot on his first day of work. The testimony notes that Jewish prisoners were subjected to systemic murder through starvation, doctor-administered poisoning, and physical abuse. Despite organized anti-fascist resistance, the SS consistently maintained the worst conditions for Jewish inmates.

In February 1941, 400 young Dutch Jews arrived at Buchenwald. SS authorities, unable to carry out a mass execution due to organized prisoner resistance, transferred them to Camp Gusen (Mauthausen), where all 400 were murdered within eight days. That same year, a rare incident of witness testimony occurred when Oberscharführer Abraham drowned prisoner Hamber in a puddle. Hamber's brother openly testified to the murder during an official inquiry. In response, Oberscharführer Petrik forced the work group's foreman to add his own name to a list of 28 workers slated for reprisal.

Following his testimony, Hamber's brother stated he expected to be killed but hoped his report would deter future camp murders. At approximately 9 o'clock, he was interrogated by Kommandant Koch, the Adjutant, the camp leader, and the Rapportführer. Koch promised the witness immunity in exchange for the truth, and the witness repeated his statement. The witness returned to his block at 9:30 but was summoned to the cell block at 11:30, where he died four days later.

The witness states that until 1942, no Jewish prisoner survived detention in Buchenwald's cell block. All 29 members of the Hamber work group were systematically transferred to the cells one by one and killed on their fourth day of detention. To cover up the murders, camp staff attempted to disguise their group membership by transferring them to other details a day or two prior to cell confinement. One injured prisoner was taken to the cells on a stretcher on Sommer's orders. A Baltic shoemaker named Löwitus escaped this fate via emigration to the Soviet Union.

Rhineland antifascist Jochen Pickard confessed to political crimes while at Dachau to secure a transfer to a penitentiary. Upon completing his sentence, he was sent to Buchenwald, where SS staff targeted him for his previous refusal to confess to camp allegations. He was placed in the cell block shortly after arrival and killed. In 1940, Ernst Heilmann, the Social Democratic Party President of the Prussian Parliament, was confined to the cells without charge and died shortly thereafter.

Kurt Eisner, son of the Bavarian Minister-President, was murdered in the cell block after refusing to testify against a fellow prisoner during an interrogation. In winter 1941, Nazi medical staff initiated typhus experiments on groups of ten and fifty Jewish prisoners. The experiments were halted for Jewish inmates due to racial regulations prohibiting the injection of Jewish blood serum into Aryan patients. In early 1942, camp physician Dr. Hoven selected approximately 400 unfit Jewish prisoners for transfer to a poison gas installation under the guise of an invalid transport.

One year after the invalid transports, the camp registry was ordered to record the 400 deportees as deceased. Dr. Hoven threatened prisoner-physician Weingärtner of Mannheim with execution if the secret was revealed. Weingärtner secretly falsified the transport lists to save numerous lives. The witness frames these actions as part of a structured resistance effort to protect key cadres, which culminated in the camp's liberation on April 11, 1945. The deportations were conducted over four days, using three trucks to remove thirty selected prisoners each morning.

On the final day of the invalid transports, sick prisoners were loaded directly from the hospital wearing only shirts. On October 17, 1942, all Jewish prisoners in Buchenwald were deported to the Auschwitz liquidation camp, except for 200 skilled armaments workers including the witness. Camp conditions for the remaining workers improved due to the reduction of camp antisemitism by organized anti-fascists and the SS's need for skilled labor. In the summer of 1943, a new crisis arose involving SS-Scharführers Schmidt and Greuel, Arbeitsführer Simons, and Rapportführer Hofschulte.
